Prompt: Write a short thank-you note to someone you have not yet thanked and would like to acknowledge. (We encourage you to share this note with that person, if possible, and reflect on the experience!) (150-200 words)*
Response:
To Ms. Sherman,
I last saw you 10 years ago, as I left West Mercer Elementary School. I still remember Monday 10AM writing periods: "Sid, just because you're smart doesn't mean everyone will know what's going on in your head," you used to remind me after yet another week of essentially no progress. But somehow, weeks of essentially no progress added up to a small seed of love for writing, and evidence in my arguments. In the last decade, I've grown to love writing (although college essays may be too much). I've even begun drafting my own time travel novel!
If you remember, you also gave me one of my most memorable nicknames. Back then the Seahawks had a dominant running back, Marshawn Lynch (AKA Beast), and you used to call me Beast Mode. Despite my small figure, when the bell rang, I'd run and push through the crowd to get to PE, music class, or recess. You taught me to be patient, to wait my turn, or to (wink wink) weave through the crowd without bumping into anyone. Thank you for believing that a STEM kid such as myself could not only be average but in fact brilliant at writing. Thank you for teaching me that to be patient isn't the same as being unproductive.
Sincerely,
Siddharth "Beast Mode" Yenamandra

Prompt: We want to be sure we’re considering your application in the context of your personal experiences: What aspects of your background (your identity, your school setting, your community, your household, etc.) have most shaped how you see yourself engaging in Northwestern’s community, be it academically, extracurricularly, culturally, politically, socially, or otherwise? (300 words max)
What is a concept that blew your mind or baffled you when you first encountered it?
Response:
π measures the ratio between a circle’s diameter and its circumference; e measures the ratio between instantaneous growth rate and its current value; i is quite literally an imaginary number. Combine these and you get Euler’s Identity: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0.

As Euler’s Identity combines the 5 most important constants in such a simple way, I believed it to be the most beautiful equation of all time. I also believed it to be false. How can combining two numbers that have infinite digits and another number that isn’t real lead to a constant like -1?

I scoured through Math StackExchange and random videos about the ‘imaginary’ unit circle; I learnt about Euler’s Formula, from which Euler’s Identity is derived; I learnt about trajectories through the complex plane. Now, whenever I look at it, I still find a sense of disbelief, not about the veritas of Euler’s Identity, but at how humanity was able to discover something so simple and pure about the mathematical laws that make up our universe.

Prompt: Why did you choose your proposed area of interest? If you selected ‘other’, what topics are you interested in pursuing?* 200
Response:
My journey with interdisciplinary research first started during early mornings, coding algorithms that learnt about properties of cancer drugs repurposed for Alzheimer’s, while I learnt about activation and loss functions. My next model generated drugs that helped with Alzheimer’s. At RISE, I learnt to model neuron interactions through circuit schematics. Eventually, I derived an index that predicted Parkinson’s likelihood.
At the same time, my hands itched to do something more than type, so I co-developed a physical device that reduced toxic waste from power plants. I connected tubes, attached arduino boards and calibrated pH sensors. After ordering parts, testing chemistry in our classroom, and building through the night, we had an initial testable prototype.
At Caltech, I hope to work in the Andersen Neuroscience Lab, using neural decoding techniques to study how neuron clusters represent movement, while exploring cortical adaptation through Caltech’s neuroengineering seminars. In CS/EE/BE 189, I’d apply machine learning to biological datasets, and in BE 150, I’d build a foundation in cellular and molecular systems relevant to neural repair.
Bioengineering allows me to disrupt different industries while doing what I love - biology and building - and there’s no place I’d rather study it at than Caltech.

Prompt: Tell us how you initially found your interest and passion for science or for a particular STEM topic, and how you have pursued or developed your interest or passion over the last few years.
Response:
My interest in neuroscience began in 10th grade with my newly created blog - Behind The Brain (BTB). I began writing several posts on complex neuroscience topics and current research while simultaneously improving BTB’s SEO. Researching a post for BTB led me to discover drug discovery problems related to increasing timeframe/cost, and a potential solution: ML models.  

After immersing myself in Medium posts on activation functions, and SVMs, I coded a local model on my 10 year old Macbook that predicted whether cancer drugs could be repurposed for Alzheimer’s treatment. The following year (11th), with a better computer, and an extra year of studying neural networks through DeepFindr videos and William Hamilton’s book, “Graph Representation Learning,” I created a new model, ABIGAIL: Amyloid-Beta Inhibition via Graph AI Learning, a graph neural network that generated novel drugs for Alzheimer’s.  

This past summer, I interned at ThinkNeuro, using R programming to study neuronal resilience, and at BU RISE, where I derived a Parkinson’s prediction index, and learnt to model neurons clusters in Python. We have modeled computers after the brain, and used computers to model the brain. The two fields are so intertwined and I love testing how we can use both of them to advance both neuroscience and computer science.

Prompt: Teach: If you could teach a class on any topic or concept, what would it be and why?
Response:
CHE-55: Middlegame Masterclass

Course Description: Life is quite similar to a chess match. The opening is filled with rules we have to follow. The strategy for the endgame is based on our past moves. But the best path to take through the middlegame isn’t quite clear. In this course, we will learn various techniques on identifying and correcting weaknesses in our position, securing our king, and most importantly, coming up with the best game plan possible. Remember, as is life, so is a chess match: not its start or end, but rather its messy middle, gives it meaning.

Prompt: Regardless of your STEM interest listed above, take this opportunity to nerd out and talk to us about whatever STEM rabbit hole you have found yourself falling into. Be as specific or broad as you would like. (Min:50/Max:150 words)
Response: Used in radios, data compression, and signal processing, the Fourier Transform reveals the frequency components of input signals. I discovered it while researching my deepest fear: Alzheimer's dementia. Dementia is terrifying. Forgetting my family, losing muscle memory, and forgetting my past feels like losing myself. Luckily, evolution protected us by wrapping up our memories in Fourier waves. Each memory is encoded by waves of neural activity; only by adding these waves up through a reverse Fourier Transform do we see the full memory! Hence, when neurons that make up individual waves die out, the entire memory isn’t wiped away. Each month, I get an update from Google Scholar on Alzheimer’s, and feel excited when I see ‘Fourier Transform’ as a keyword. Reading such papers, I’ve decided to join the diverse community of physicists, mathematicians, biologists, and programmers trying to protect the Fourier waves of memory in all of our heads.

Prompt: How did you manage a situation or challenge that you didn’t expect? What did you learn from it?
Response:
Practice makes perfect, especially for rocketry. On the day of our official launch, remembering this aphorism, I decided to take a practice run first. The test went perfectly, but as we set up for the real flight, raindrops began to fall. With a storm looming and our deadline days away, I urged our team to move fast. Betelgeuse lifted off smoothly and reached its apogee right on schedule - but rather than unfurling its parachute, it began to plummet and crumpled into the earth. In our hurry, we'd forgotten to untangle the parachute wires.
The next day, I led a team meeting, delegating roles for different rocket components. In two days, using spare parts, we assembled Betelgeuse 2.0 and raced to the launch site within four hours of the deadline. Remembering how the earlier crash was caused by a practice run that wasted time, I decided to skip the practice run. We launched successfully, but missed the cutoff by 0.5 points.
From this, I’ve learnt to balance precision with practicality. Whether I’m coding an AI model or writing a blog post, I ask myself: is one more iteration actually leading to progress? And if not, I remember my crumpled rocket, and decide that it’s time to take a leap of faith.

Prompt: MIT brings people with diverse backgrounds together to collaborate, from tackling the world's biggest challenges to lending a helping hand. Describe one way you have collaborated with others to learn from them, with them, or contribute to your community together.* 225 words or fewer
Response: It takes a village to teach chess. After school, I race through traffic, barely arriving on time to the Chess4Life Issaquah center: 3:58 PM. Stanley, my manager, hands me the stack of worksheets I asked him to print out. My first lesson is on ‘forks’. I teach the basics before puzzles, counting to ten before letting anyone raise their hand, giving slower students time to think without pressure. While they do worksheets, I notice a peculiar silence from Kyden's classroom. Between lessons, Kyden mentions he borrowed my counting trick. "Ah! Explains the strange silence,” I joke. We end up discussing AI (he trains models part-time) debating whether pattern recognition or positional understanding is more important for training chess-playing models. Stanley overhears and joins in. "Definitely positional. In fact, we should start adding position concepts to lessons." My second lesson is on pins, a tactic. Kyden suggests his "freeze chess" game to incorporate positional theory: a game where students can ‘immobilize/freeze’ an opponent piece for one turn, thus teaching them the positional importance of a pin. It works. "Nice positional touch," Stanley says while stopping by to hand me some worksheets. As I leave, I agree to sub for Samuel tomorrow. Driving home, I realize what makes Chess4Life work: we're all pieces on the same chessboard, helping each other teach chess.

Prompt: In what ways have you done something different than what was expected in your educational journey?
Response:
March 2020. The pandemic has just begun and I can only play so much table tennis. I open up Khan Academy upon my mum’s imploration to study Geometry, but to spite her, I begin “AP Physics 1”. Over the next two weeks, whatever little physics I learn from Khan Academy, I apply to my ping-pong trickshots: I learn to play dropshots by absorbing the ball’s energy and to my and my brother’s awe, I curve the ball sideways in the air by using the Magnus effect and Bernoulli’s Principle.

December 2022. I’ve lost most of my chess students, despite my low hourly rate ($15), so during winter break, I decide to go through “Microeconomics”, a subject I know nothing about. Importantly, I learn about luxury goods - goods that have an increasing demand with increasing price. Over the next 3 months, I price my lessons higher ($35-40/hr) and surprisingly, more students reach out asking for lessons.

“Pixar in a Box” taught me storytelling concepts I’m using in my time-travel based novel; “Organic Chemistry” planted the seeds for NNANO, my device that manages toxic powerplant pollution; “Khan Academy + PERT’s Growth Mindset” helped me captain my rocketry team through two crashes. Clicking on eclectic [a]Khan Academy courses has taught me not just information, but also the ability to adapt what I’ve learnt to fit different situations.

Prompt: We know you lead a busy life, full of activities, many of which are required of you. Tell us about something you do simply for the pleasure of it.*
Response:
"Behind the back - let's go!" my brother calls out, serving low and fast. I spin around, paddle behind my back, and swing blind. The ball rockets past his ear into the wall, as we both laugh at my flailing attempt.

Table tennis has always been the glue between us. In my 7th grade, we cut out cardboard rackets and a net to play on our dining table, adding dried-out cellotape as a makeshift rubber to create spin. As I started high school, we got a proper table, rackets, and balls, and we quickly became obsessed. We began watching Pongfinity, trying to recreate their trickshots: behind the back, no look, around the net. But I don’t play with the correct technique, and I go for shots that I have no place trying, because I don’t play table tennis to win. I play to have fun with my brother, to talk with my brother, and to occasionally hit that trickshot.

The orange ball whizzes off the blue table, past my outstretched arm. I pick up the ball and continue to rally and talk with my brother. Table tennis has always been the glue between us. In my 7th grade, we cut out cardboard rackets and a net to play on our dining table, adding dried-out cellotape as a makeshift rubber to create spin. My freshman year, we got a proper table, rackets, and balls, and quickly became obsessed. We began watching Pongfinity, trying to recreate their trickshots: behind the back, no look, around the net. But I don’t play with the correct technique, and I go for shots that I have no place trying, because I don’t play table tennis to win. I play to laugh with my brother, to relax with my brother, and to occasionally hit that trickshot - and scream my head off in excitement with my brother.

Prompt: Princeton values community and encourages students, faculty, staff and leadership to engage in respectful conversations that can expand their perspectives and challenge their ideas and beliefs. As a prospective member of this community, reflect on how your lived experiences will impact the conversations you will have in the classroom, the dining hall or other campus spaces. What lessons have you learned in life thus far? What will your classmates learn from you? In short, how has your lived experience shaped you? (500 words or fewer)*
Response: Growing up Telugu, my family and I have watched countless Telugu films, and I’ve noticed some common motifs. They always follow a Hero’s Journey storyline, with a hit soundtrack helping the hero face his final challenge in the ‘Resurrection’ phase of the 3rd Act. I’ve learnt to use these soundtracks in my own life, playing them when I’m stuck with a writing block, or a tricky coding bug. Telugu movies also always feature a community of friends, sticking with the hero through fights (and dance scenes). Over the last four years, I’ve found my own community, a community not of aunties and uncles dancing to older songs, but of kids my age: Aryan, who sent me Telugu reels and memes; Vedant, who made incredible dosas; and Rahoul, who joined in with my Telugu humming. While studying for APs, Aryan’s Telugu jokes kept our spirits up. While building NNANO, Vedant cooked dosas while I made the chutney, and we all enjoyed a much-needed break. While going on runs, Rahoul and I shared our Telugu song playlists with each other. Despite all these moments of connection, some of the most interesting moments came through confusion: Aryan sending a reel with a Telugu reference I didn’t understand, Vedant not recognizing the chutney I made, and Rahoul and I realizing we listen to Telugu music from different decades. These feelings of disconnection, despite our Telugu connection, showed me how truly different we all are. If my Telugu friends, who speak my language, watch the same movies and eat the same food, still have completely different reference points, then how much more do people from entirely different backgrounds? As I started building friendships beyond my Telugu circle - Anuraag from Tamilnadu, who taught me to make vethe kozhumbu and Rain Jia, who taught me a tennis kick serve and how to make dumplings while I taught him how to make methi chapathi - I learnt to look for points of commonalities other than just culture. At Princeton, I’ll look for points of connection in each conversation, whether someone is also interested in NeuroAI research or obsessed with Federer. At the same time, I’ll deep dive into our differences, not just asking about their family traditions I’m not familiar with, but also those traditions’ origins, and how those traditions have impacted them. I'd also love to share what being Telugu has taught me. I’ll play my Telugu soundtracks during late-night study sessions, regaling my peers with the whole backstory to that soundtrack. And I hope as an upperclassman, I can cook in a communal kitchen dorm, teaching my family’s methi chapathi and Anuraag’s vethe kozhumba recipes. Growing up Telugu in America has taught me to approach every person knowing they're carrying their own complete perspective and background. At Princeton, I’ll share my own soundtrack - including its backstory - while learning to hum along to everyone else’s.

Prompt: Please describe why you are interested in studying engineering at Princeton. Include any of your experiences in, or exposure to engineering, and how you think the programs offered at the University suit your particular interests. (Please respond in 250 words or fewer)*
Response: Engineering without practical application is futile. Engineering without creativity is boring. I've always tried to balance both - a balance that Princeton Engineering embodies through its focus on ‘engineering in the service of humanity’, a focus I take with my current research. When building ABIGAIL, I loved modeling three dimensions rather than the usual two, just one of the many ways I was creative with ABIGAIL's code. But what made ABIGAIL worthy was its impact on Alzheimer's research. Princeton's Neuroscience Institute (PNI), with its truly unique interdisciplinary approach, shares this same creative practicality, modelling neural circuits to understand everything from decision-making to memory formation. I am especially interested in PNI’s NeuroAI division, where I hope to learn how biologically inspired architectures can reshape how artificial systems process and store information, hypothetically reducing AI’s energy usage and pollution. I'd also join Princeton's student-led engineering teams, channeling my rocketry spirit by joining Princeton Rocketry. I’d also join iGEM, working collaboratively with others interested in using synthetic biology to help cure diseases, learning undergraduate conference presentation techniques from the most experienced Princeton upperclassmen. Independently, I’d utilize the Micro/Nano Fabrication Center (MNFC) to develop new drug-delivery systems using magnetic nanotechnology and build physical neural sensors. Princeton offers opportunities at the intersection between innovation and practicality, and I know Princeton Engineering will help me balance my curiosity and creativity with purposefulness, developing technologies and tools that will meaningfully advance medicine.

Prompt: Princeton has a longstanding commitment to understanding our responsibility to society through service and civic engagement. How does your own story intersect with these ideals? (250 words or fewer)*
Response: In the summer of 2024, I boarded the lone corner seat on a mobile hospital van in Puttaparthi, Andhra Pradesh, India. For the next two weeks, I volunteered in a different village. Each day, I set up makeshift clinics in schoolyards with other volunteers. I took vitals, measured blood sugar levels, packed medication bags, and guided patients from station to station. But my biggest impacts were through my Telugu translations. These, although far from perfect, helped when elders couldn't understand the formal Puttaparthi dialect or when patients were confused by English medical terms. But even as an unofficial ‘translator’, there were times I struggled. While I squinted to read doctors' scribbled notes, I saw villagers confused by the Puttaparthi dialect. On Day 6, I heard an old man tell his daughter, "I don't understand when to take these medicines; the doctors don't make sense." In him, I saw myself during my first days in India, unable to understand the dialect and scared to ask. Back in Seattle, I designed a sticker system for dosage instructions: a sun and food sticker meant "after breakfast," while a moon and water droplet meant "before bed with water." I emailed prototypes to Director Narasimhan and gathered feedback from volunteer pharmacists. Dr. Narasimhan remains hesitant, but I keep trying. As a translator, I helped hundreds. With these stickers, I could help thousands, leaving a lasting imprint that outlasts my volunteering hours.

Prompt: Share how an aspect of your growing up has inspired or challenged you, and what unique contributions this might allow you to make to the Brown community.
Response: Growing up Telugu, my family and I have watched countless Telugu films, and I’ve noticed some common motifs. They always follow a Hero’s Journey storyline, with a hit soundtrack helping the hero face his final challenge in the ‘Resurrection’ phase of the 3rd Act. I’ve learnt to use these soundtracks in my own life, playing them when I’m stuck with a writing block, or a tricky coding bug. Telugu movies also always feature a community of friends, sticking with the hero through fights (and dance scenes). Over the last four years, I’ve found my own community, a community not of aunties and uncles dancing to older songs, but of kids my age: Abhinav the jokester, Amogh the chef, and Rahoul the runner. While studying for APs, Abhinav’s jokes kept our attitude positive. During our end-of-year sleepover, Amogh cooked dosas while I made the chutney, and our group talked about our goals. While resting between 100-meter sprints, Rahoul and I bonded over our fear of failure. Throughout the years, our friend group has become a safe space for us to discuss anything and uplift each other. At Brown, I want to bring that soundtrack energy into groups like SASA and Barsaat - helping organize cultural nights and playlist exchanges - and start “song & study” dorm sessions. I also see myself joining the PMHA program to offer the same emotional support I’ve relied on - creating trust-filled spaces in the BCSC or other student‑life hubs where we can share our emotions, fears, and aspirations.

Prompt: Discuss your interest in medicine and how the Brown PLME program aligns with your goals.
Response: Despite how much I love Harry Potter, by the time I was 13, I’d learnt that wizards aren't real. So, I looked for the second closest thing: physicians. Physicians give patients potions (prescriptions for medicines) and cure them. Physicians wave their wands (scalpels and scissors) and remove tumors from brains. And most importantly, just like Harry, physicians give people hope in tough situations. Harry changed people's lives through magic. As a physician, I'd change them through science and surgery.        I believe that impacting others is what gives our lives meaning. As a physician, I will impact every patient I interact with. To me, the chance to save even a single person is enough to become a physician. But I want to be a neurosurgeon, who quite literally pulls patients from the jaws of death. In fact, neurosurgeons can save over 100 lives per year. I also know that as a neurosurgeon, I’d never get bored; No brain is the exact same, and so no surgery would be the exact same. And with the field of neuroscience constantly growing, I know I’d never stop learning. The path to becoming a neurosurgeon is a long journey, but the Brown PLME program would allow me to begin it sooner. By earning my MD through an integrated eight-year pathway directly connected to Brown’s open curriculum, I could move directly into medical school without the gap year most applicants take. Since neurosurgery is what I want to dedicate my life to, I hope to begin that journey at Brown. I would focus on computational biology, researching through projects in the Carney Institute for Brain Science, working in the Center for Neurobiology of Cells and Circuits or the Brown Multimodal Neuroimaging Core, using neuroimaging and electrophysiology to study neural circuits and surgical targets, similar to my research at RISE. The Webb Lab’s research on reactivating neural stem cells seems very exciting, especially its potential in reversing dementia, and surprisingly, developing personalized medicine. Furthermore, through Project Sunshine, I could teach with pediatric patients chess, complementing clinical shadowing in the Department of Neurosurgery at Rhode Island Hospital. While caring for children, I’d be able to give them back some of that joy and innocence that disease robbed them of. On the technical side, at The Warren Alpert Medical School, I would have access to rotations in brain tumor, spinal, and cerebrovascular surgery, as well as access to Brown’s neurosurgical and neuroanatomy simulation labs, allowing me to practice microsurgery and stereotactic techniques. Neurosurgery is the field in medicine with the most opportunity for discovery. I want to be at the frontier of this discovery, forging the way for the next generation of neurologists/surgeons while giving as many patients as I can hope in their future. Brown PLME is where I can start this journey, equipping me to make a real impact as a neurologist and surgeon.

Prompt: Brown students care deeply about their work and the world around them. Students find contentment, satisfaction, and meaning in daily interactions and major discoveries. Whether big or small, mundane or spectacular, tell us about something that brings you joy. (250 word limit)
Response:
I try to work on my time-travel sci-fi novel every night. My face illuminated only by the glow of my MacBook, I get lost in ‘Stonebridge’ (the setting of my novel), while trying to write a character much smarter than myself. Surprisingly, writing my novel has also strengthened my bond with my brother. Testing whether certain clues in my mystery subplot make sense with my brother has given us an arsenal of secret codes between ourselves: “hollow” means our mum is in a bad mood, comparing her to a quite strict character, Ms. Holloway, and “airvent” means ‘listen carefully’, referring to how the protagonist is caught eavesdropping through air vents in the first chapter.

Despite this, I mostly write for myself. Writing my novel has taught me a lot about myself: I’m a passionate plotter and I hate dishonesty, even in the characters I’ve created. Yet, while writing my novel, I rarely follow the outline I’ve created to a dot. Instead, I go on a journey, exploring a world while creating it, only referring to the outline when I’m stuck. Writing feels like a conversation between my imagination and myself, where I can dream, and remember those dreams, and revise those dreams. More importantly, writing gives me a space to lose myself and find myself, and there lies a great joy in both.

Prompt: If you could teach a class on any one thing, whether academic or otherwise, what would it be? (100 word limit)
Response:
CHE-55: Middlegame Masterclass

Course Description: Life is quite similar to a chess match. The opening is filled with rules we have to follow. The strategy for the endgame is based on our past moves. But the best path to take through the middlegame isn’t quite clear. In this course, we will learn various techniques on identifying and correcting weaknesses in our position, securing our king, and most importantly, coming up with the best game plan possible. Remember, as is life, so is a chess match: not its start or end, but rather its messy middle, that gives it meaning.

Prompt: Brown's Open Curriculum allows students to explore broadly while also diving deeply into their academic pursuits. Tell us about any academic interests that excite you, and how you might pursue them at Brown. (250 word limit)*
Response:
My journey with computational neuroscience first started during early mornings, coding algorithms that learnt about properties of drugs that inhibited Alzheimer’s, while I learnt about activation and loss functions. At RISE, I learnt to model neuron interactions through circuit schematics. Eventually, I derived an index that predicted Parkinson’s likelihood.

During my high school, I also started a neuroscience blog, Behind the Brain, as a way to relax while learning. I interviewed neuroscience professors and researchers, and researched and wrote posts on basic topics and recent news. Recently, I’ve started to write a time-travel based novel, shifting some of my writing to a more creative outlet, one that allows me to relax while exploring a completely different mindset.

Brown allows all these interests to coexist. Building from my experience at RISE, I'd contribute to CLPS and NEUR labs that use machine learning to analyze neural data, perhaps introducing a focus into social media addiction. Courses like CLPS 1520 (Computational Cognitive Neuroscience), CSCI 1470 (Deep Learning), would teach me to build better predictive models for neurodegeneration, building on my prior experience building AI models. At the same time, fiction workshops like LITR 1110, would help me grow as a writer through mini-challenges (writing a story on a Mobius Strip seems fun!). I see a future for myself in which I’m a physician who researches neurodegenerative diseases, codes neural networks and models, and writes fiction, and Brown University’s Open Curriculum is the best path I see leading to this future.

Prompt: How has your personal background uniquely shaped your perspective on the field of medicine?
Response: Placebo effect, translation inequities. Difficulties with communication in India, in Spokane, with Spanish-speaking patients and patients with cognitive challenges. Shaped not only your awareness of these inequities, but the importance of bringing. patients into decision-making, paying attention to each patient’s humanity, not just symptoms/disease. Inequities intersect here. In India, doctors don’t set up in villages, don’t create sustainable practices in villages, b/c you can’t make enough money to become a doctor and work there. It’s a bit iffy, also, b/c of the caste system, and these villages often have populations with lower castes, and too often some doctors from higher castes, more education, will have a sense of superiority, rush through. In Spokane, probably more racial, also economic. Certain patients can afford things, certain patients can’t. Second-biggest city in Washington, at the same time, driving down the road, see ten people taking drugs in the street in the middle of a weekday. Can also be racial judgment, some nurses might rush treatment of Spanish patients compared to white patients. Leads to differences, even subtle ones, in quality of care. Everywhere I volunteered, I was considered an outsider. In India, I was an outsider for the graphic tees I wore, for the slight stumble in my Telugu conjugations. And in Spokane, I was an outsider for my brown skin, for the fact that I lived in Seattle, and for my Indian accent. But being considered an outsider comes with its own advantages. While volunteering in the Andhra Pradesh villages, I’d often hear patients complaining that they couldn’t understand the doctor’s Telugu dialect, and they didn’t understand the science behind medicine. I empathized with these villagers because I sometimes struggled to understand the doctors. But who was to blame for this miscommunication? I eventually realized that the problem spanned decades back. These villages had become so unprofitable for clinics, that they couldn’t afford to open here. This has caused future generations to grow up without much healthcare, thus widening the knowledge gap between cities and rural villages, perpetuating the problem. In Spokane, I saw nurses shy away from Mexican patients, spending less time in their rooms, similar to how they avoided asking me for help in favor of a more local volunteer. I saw these patients shy away from asking questions, choosing to ask me instead, in their eyes, a fellow outsider. With this outsider view into healthcare, I’ve realized, all patients should feel like they are the most important. Not only does this increase their confidence in the healthcare system, but it also boosts their confidence in their own survival and recovery. And saving as many patients as possible is what healthcare is all about.

Prompt: What about being a student at BU most excites you? How do you hope to contribute to our campus community?
Response:
Every day at BU RISE, I’d catch the 8:30 AM Green Line subway. During the day, I’d research Parkinson's in the Metcalf Science Center, with lunch at Marciano’s. But at RISE, I wasn’t just a researcher; I was an explorer. I studied neural circuits and subway maps; I learned about RNAi and played volleyball at Assembly Row; and at night, I admired the stars and the city skyline. 
BU’s emphasis on early research engagement, something I saw clearly during RISE, is what excites me most. I could join the Neurophotonics Center or the Center for Systems Neuroscience to study addiction-related circuitry, joining as a neural circuit modeller, building off of skills I learnt at RISE. But of course, BU is more than just a place for research. I’d continue the tradition I started during RISE of walking to Insomnia Cookies with my friends each Saturday night, I’d play volleyball in the various lounges, and most importantly, I’d carry my chess board in my backpack. I remember playing blitz games in the Rich Hall Lounge. Next year, at BU, I’m excited to continue playing these blitz games at Mugar Memorial Library, and late-night matches in my Rich Hall or Warren Towers dorm. 
Apart from just BU, the Boston chess scene is very active. I’d join the Boylston Chess Foundation, learning from the grandmasters that visit their meetings. Using my experience in teaching chess, I’d look for work at Chess Wizards, and volunteer in my free time by teaching chess through Street Chess, a local Boston NGO that focuses on bridging gaps through chess. 
Next fall, I hope to be back in BU, this time for a bit longer, to build stronger relationships, engage in more research, and connect with the city of Boston through chess.

Prompt: If you had 20 minutes to pitch a transformational idea (an idea that affects or would influence a significant percentage of the populace), what would you pitch and why? Explain your thought process. Are there similar solutions already established and how might your idea enhance those solutions? How would this idea change the current state of things? How would you leverage your personal experiences and professional goals to make this idea a reality?
Response: I would pitch FreeCode, an intuitive IDE (integrated development environment) that, instead of processing only lines of code, would interpret typed text, diagrams, scribbles, even drawings, capturing the intent of the user without requiring memorized syntax. In other words, it would let people communicate ideas to a computer the way they already think, whether that’s through words, visuals, or sketches. I imagine a world where a kid could draw out an idea for a game the way they’d sketch in a notebook, or where someone in a small town without a coding teacher could build a tool that makes their life easier. I imagine classrooms where coding is treated not as a technical skill for a few, but as a creative outlet for everyone, similar to writing or painting. In the last 30 years, complex coding algorithms have completely changed the world, from the ‘Cloud’, to encryption. But learning to code complex algorithms has an extremely high barrier to entry. It requires not only a teacher who understands logic, but also one who can explain the “grammar” of code: syntax rules, formatting, and exceptions. Furthermore, to fully build an application, you often need to juggle multiple languages, each with its own quirks. When I was working on my model, ABIGAIL, I had to use R to break down chemical data and Python to construct the neural network. Sometimes, I felt like I was translating between dialects I hadn’t fully mastered. I wasn’t limited by my ideas, but by the steep learning curve of syntax. FreeCode would solve all these problems by allowing anyone to code in whatever medium they preferred - visual, written, even auditory - without harping on syntax or format. In this world, you wouldn’t have to start as a “coder” before you become a “creator.” Anyone, anywhere, with nothing more than curiosity and imagination, could turn their thoughts into something real. Currently, there are about five million casual and professional developers in the U.S. With FreeCode, that number could grow exponentially. Seventy-eight percent of U.S. adults own computers, and suddenly, every one of them could have a way into coding. With an exponential increase in the number of algorithms worldwide, creation itself would increase. More people coding means more tools being built (apps, websites, algorithms) to solve problems we haven’t even imagined yet. And when we change the way people approach coding, allowing them to sketch, write, or diagram instead of memorizing commands, we change the way they approach problems. Suddenly, the act of coding isn’t reserved for those with access to the right teachers or enough patience to master syntax. It becomes something natural, something intuitive. This shift would be especially important for communities and areas where coding instruction isn’t easily accessible. Today, even if someone can teach themselves syntax, they still need guidance to understand the deeper logic behind programming. FreeCode users could experiment and discover on their own, drawing out their ideas and watching them come to life. Instead of struggling through the reputation of coding as “boring” or “difficult,” children could jump straight into the fun part: designing. However, actualizing FreeCode won’t be as simple as throwing together a couple of diagrams and complementing with some code. Truly creating FreeCode would require building an AI capable of interpreting not just words, but the intention behind a sketch or pseudocode. Models like GPT and Claude already convert text into functional code; FreeCode would take this a step further, training AI to recognize visual logic and translate it into runnable programs. Considering the rate of AI progress in visual construction and decomposition, audio to transcription, and the exponentially increasing amount of investment into the AI market, it is likely that within 7-12 years, this type of technology will be available. This whole process will require engineers and data scientists along with investors and product visionaries. I see myself leading the development of FreeCode by visualizing the final product, while supporting the team by pitching to venture capitalists. With my rudimentary knowledge in AI, I could also check in with the engineering team, making sure we meet investor milestones. Eventually, I know we will be able to release FreeCode and watch it change the world forever.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked for an explanation of how the applicant's interests and experiences align with the opportunities and values of a specific medical school's program.
Response:
While touring biotech companies during my time at RISE, I asked them whether they were looking into Alzheimer’s treatments. Nearly 80% said no. Their reasoning was consistent: Alzheimer’s research is too slow, too costly, and too uncertain to attract investment. For me, this was upsetting. After I had researched for months on Alzheimer’s drug generation, I knew the impact biotech companies could have on the millions of families that struggle with Alzheimer’s, and I knew I wanted to change the mindset the biotech industry had towards Alzheimer’s research. The Hippocratic Oath isn’t just for the hospital; it’s a lifestyle that I’d adopt as a physician. Hence, I’d care for my patients when I’m wearing the white coat, AND when I’m not. While wearing the white coat, I'd perform surgeries, and while I’m not, I’d advocate for increased Alzheimer’s funding, and start research of my own.

George Washington University’s SMHS allows me to begin caring long before earning my white coat. Through the Clinical Public Health Curriculum, I’d learn how policy decisions, funding, and social determinants indirectly shape patient outcomes through grants and legislation. The Rodham Institute would allow me to work directly with communities in DC, particularly Wards 7 and 8, where chronic disease and maternal mortality rates remain high. Rather than studying disparities in textbooks, I could help design community-based interventions in collaboration with residents themselves. Lastly, through the Health Equity Scholars Program I’d research how systemic neglect (like the lack of investment in Alzheimer’s) translates into local inequities in patient care. DC itself offers opportunities no other city can. Through GW’s student-run free clinics, I could provide direct care to underserved patients while learning from organizations like Whitman-Walker Health and Unity Health Care, which model innovative care for HIV patients, and the unhoused. I volunteer at Deaconess Hospital in Spokane, WA, and by taking vitals, moving, and talking to the patients, I’ve built skills that can greatly help me volunteer in GW’s free clinics. And in the nation’s capital, I can share patient stories to agencies like the NIH and HRSA, allowing me to fight for increased funding for underrepresented diseases like Alzheimer’s. When I think back to those biotech tours, I realize that solving problems like Alzheimer’s requires more than scientific breakthroughs; it requires physicians who amplify suffering patients’ voices. At GW, I will gain the skills to fight for those voices, whether in the exam room, in DC communities, or at the policy table. With my white coat, as a physician, I’ll ensure that every patient, no matter their condition or how overlooked their disease is, knows they have an advocate in me.

Prompt: If you had the power to change the course of history in your community or the world, what would you do and why?
Response:
March 2020. Each day, I’d wake up and look up ‘Covid deaths in the US’, and smile as the map showed I was safe. I’d play chess with my brother, and run around the house. But as the weeklong vacation stretched into a year, and the red dots showing outbreaks inched closer towards Seattle, WA, I began to feel scared. Luckily, online school gave me an avenue to distract myself from the horrors of COVID.

March 2021. As part of the second semester of 8th Science Honors, I had to present on HIV/AIDS. I collected mortality rates, and infection pathways. Initially, all I focused on was the rubric, but as the printed reports scattered on my desk of increased anti-LGBTQ violence during the 80s began to remind me of the anti-Asian violence being covered on CNN, I started to feel scared of not just the virus, but of other humans.

April 2021. Presentation day. I unmuted myself, staring at an array of blank screens, apart from my teacher’s smiling face. I covered the mechanism of HIV, how it spread, mortality rates, and concluded with its impact on the LGBTQ community, earning all the points, but I wasn’t finished. On my last slide, all I had written was ‘History repeats itself’. I explained the patterns I had found between the violence during the HIV pandemic and COVID, speaking passionately until the teacher muted me through Teams.

That HIV/AIDS project taught me that nobody is safe from the injustice faced by one. The violence endured during the 1980s, the misinformation spread about HIV, and the government’s inability to educate the public - artifacts from the past (or so I’d believed) - had returned 40 years later. If I could change history, I would ensure that early warnings about HIV/AIDS were met with urgency rather than dismissal. I would see that government leaders spoke honestly, that scientists received immediate support and funding from the NIH, and that communities were empowered to protect themselves without fear of judgment or violence. And perhaps, this history would’ve repeated itself in 2020.

Unfortunately, I can’t change history, but at GWU, I see a path to shape the future of healthcare justice. Through GWU’s Bioengineering program and Global Health Studies, I can explore how data-based and engineering solutions intersect with public health, from designing affordable diagnostic tools to improving vaccine distribution systems. Participating in Epidemiology & Public Health initiatives will give me hands-on experience analyzing health data, understanding community needs, and designing interventions that prevent both disease and discrimination. Beyond the classroom, D.C. provides access to organizations like the NIH, CDC, and NGOs like PSI and Project HOPE, which I can partner with to help HIV prevention and digitization of health systems. Through GWU, I will turn my knowledge into bioengineering innovations and advocacy, creating a future where an eighth grader in 2100 studying 21st-century healthcare equity will hope history repeats itself because the past was made so just and equitable.

Prompt: Describe a time when you engaged others in meaningful dialogue around an issue that was important to you. Did this exchange create change, new perspectives, or deeper relationships?
Response:
During my junior year of high school, I had the opportunity to lead a discussion on climate change in my environmental science class. The topic was not only important to me, but it was also a pressing issue that affected our community and the world at large. I wanted to create a space where my classmates could express their thoughts and feelings about climate change, and hopefully inspire them to take action.

I began by presenting some alarming statistics about rising global temperatures and the impact of climate change on our local environment. I could see the concern on my classmates' faces, but I also noticed a sense of helplessness. Many felt that the problem was too big for any individual to tackle. I wanted to shift that mindset, so I encouraged an open dialogue where everyone could share their opinions and experiences related to the issue.

As we discussed, I asked my classmates to think about how climate change affected their lives personally. One student shared how her family’s farm had been impacted by unpredictable weather patterns, while another spoke about the increase in allergies and respiratory issues in our community. Hearing these personal stories made the issue more tangible and relatable. It was clear that climate change was not just a distant problem; it was affecting us right here and now.

I then introduced the idea of local action. I proposed that we organize a community clean-up day to raise awareness about pollution and its effects on our environment. To my surprise, my classmates were enthusiastic about the idea. We brainstormed ways to promote the event, including creating posters and using social media to reach a wider audience. The energy in the room shifted from despair to empowerment, and I could see that my classmates were beginning to feel that they could make a difference.

The clean-up day was a success. We gathered a group of over fifty students and community members, and together we collected trash from local parks and waterways. The event not only beautified our community but also sparked conversations about sustainability and environmental responsibility. After the clean-up, we held a reflection session where participants shared their thoughts on the experience. Many expressed a newfound commitment to being more environmentally conscious in their daily lives.

This experience taught me the power of dialogue and community engagement. By creating a space for meaningful conversation, I was able to help my classmates see the importance of their individual actions in the fight against climate change. It also deepened my relationships with my peers, as we bonded over our shared commitment to making a positive impact. I learned that change often starts with a conversation, and I am inspired to continue fostering dialogue around important issues in the future.

Prompt: Why are you interested in attending Columbia University? We encourage you to consider the aspect(s) that you find unique and compelling about Columbia. (150 words or fewer)*
Response: It’s 3 a.m., the fireplace crackling behind me. My friends - Abhinav, Zirui, Amogh, and Rain - are crowded around a game of Exploding Kittens, but no one is paying attention. Instead, we’re debating: Can technology ever be truly objective? What does “progress” mean? Columbia’s Core Curriculum excites me because it formalizes the kind of late-night conversations I already enjoy. In Contemporary Civilization and Frontiers of Science, I’d learn about ethical responsibility and scientific uncertainty, then carry those ideas into Biomedical Engineering Design and Biomedical Signals and Systems, where I’d design a novel drug delivery system, while considering all effects of innovation. With all students reading shared texts, conversations won’t stay confined to the classroom - they’ll spill into Carman lounges after Lit Hum, into late-night study rooms in Lerner, and into lab benches in the BME Design Studio. Columbia ensures discussion is part of engineering, thus fostering collaboration and creativity.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked the applicant to discuss their relevant experiences and goals, explaining why they are interested in a particular program at a specific university and how it aligns with their academic and career aspirations.
Response:
My journey with research started during early mornings, coding algorithms that learnt about properties of cancer drugs repurposed for Alzheimer’s, while I learnt about activation and loss functions. I modelled neuron interactions at RISE, where I derived an index predicting Parkinson’s likelihood. At the same time, I co-developed a device to reduce toxic waste from power plants, iterating through physical prototypes and calibrating sensors.
Biomedical Engineering at Columbia is the clearest way for me to combine my computational research with hands-on prototyping. Bioengineering at Columbia offers a supportive environment to refine my technical skills while developing technologies that advance medicine.

Prompt: Tell us about an aspect of your life so far or your lived experience that is important to you, and describe how it has shaped the way you would learn from and contribute to Columbia's multidimensional and collaborative environment.
Response: Everyday during my 6 weeks at RISE, I’d catch the 8:30 AM Green Line subway. For the next 8 hours, I’d research Parkinson's neuronal modeling. But at RISE, I wasn’t just a researcher; I was an explorer. I studied neural circuits and subway maps; I learned about RNAi at the biotech giant Alnylam and played volleyball at Assembly Row; I admired the stars and the skyline while practicing tennis. Prompt: At Columbia, students representing a wide range of perspectives are invited to live and learn together. In such a community, questions and debates naturally arise. Please describe a time when you did not agree with someone and discuss how you engaged with them and what you took away from the interaction. (150 words or fewer)
Response:
After watching The Prestige with my brother Akhi, we slipped into our usual post-movie debate. “Who’s the better character?” I asked.

        I went with Borden, while Akhi went with Angier. I loved Borden’s ambition and dedication to his craft, while my brother liked Angier’s drive to understand Borden’s secret. I saw Angier as a corner-cutting charlatan, while Akhi saw Borden as a Machiavellianistic monster. But as we debated, I realized what I saw as dedication, Akhi saw as obsession, and what he saw as drive, I saw as fanaticism.
        
        We eventually agreed to disagree, settling into our covers. But I stayed awake. We’d interpreted the same material differently, just as scientists may interpret data differently. But why? Perhaps, I thought, our debate revealed more about us, than the characters we were discussing. Perhaps, our disagreements helped us to understand each other better.

Prompt: In college/university, students are often challenged in ways that they could not anticipate. Please describe a situation in which you have navigated through adversity and discuss how you changed as a result. (150 words or fewer)*
Response: People say to reach for the stars, because then you’ll land on the moon. But when I land on the moon, I don’t look back at Earth, I still stare at the stars. Perfectionism leaked into all aspects of my life. When I won a first place award at a science fair, I wished I’d qualified for internationals. When I made varsity doubles, I wished I’d made singles. When I hit 100,000 views on my blog, I wished for an extra 0. Although this perfectionism gave me considerable success, it also left me with a hollow feeling and eventually burnout. To get out of burnout, I began measuring my effort rather than results. I began tracking hours of coding, playing tennis, and writing for my blog. Now whenever I land on the moon, my ambition still strives towards the stars, but I appreciate how high above Earth I am.

Prompt: Describe a time when you faced a challenge, how you approached it, and what you learned from the experience.
Response:
Practice makes perfect, especially for rocketry. On the day of our official launch, remembering this aphorism, I decided to take a practice launch first. The test went perfectly, but as we set up for the real flight, raindrops began to fall. With a storm looming, I urged our team to move fast. Our rocket, Betelgeuse, lifted off smoothly, but its parachute failed, and it crashed.
The next day, I led a meeting, assigning teammates to rebuild critical parts. In two days, we assembled Betelgeuse 2.0 from spares and raced to the launch site, just hours before the deadline. Under pressure, I skipped the practice run. We launched but missed the cutoff by 0.5 points.
From this, I learned to balance perfection with practicality. Practice has its own time, but sometimes, the time you lose from practice is more valuable than the practice itself.

Prompt: We all contribute to, and are influenced by, the communities that are meaningful to us. Share how you’ve been shaped by one of the communities you belong to.
Response: Throughout my life, Telugu stories, whether the latest theater release or my parents’ childhood favorites, have shaped how I face challenges and connect with others. Growing up Telugu, I’ve watched countless Telugu films in the theater: almost always, the hero will struggle, fail, and face setbacks. After a quick intermission, my family and I watch as the hero comes back stronger, usually with a hit song or soundtrack accompanying his comeback. Over the last four years, I’ve started using these songs as personal motivators. When I hit an error in my code or a writing block, I play these songs/soundtracks and imagine myself as the hero, fighting the imaginary bugs or the mental block. Apart from Telugu movies running in theaters, our family explored films and songs from my parents’ childhood. In these older movies, I noticed how each hero had their own Telugu friend group. And slowly I began to look for those friends in my own life. Over the last four years, I’ve found my own Telugu community, a community not of aunties and uncles dancing to older songs, but of kids my age. Aryan who sent me Telugu reels and memes, Vedant who made incredible dosas, and Rahoul who joined in with my Telugu humming. While studying for APs, Aryan’s Telugu jokes kept our spirits up. While building NNANO, Vedant cooked dosas while I made the chutney, and we all enjoyed a much needed break. While going on runs, Rahoul and I shared our Telugu song playlists with each other. These moments show the impact being Telugu has had on my life. It has helped me discover myself: someone who can overcome anything with the right soundtrack. But being Telugu has also let me discover a community of friends which acts as that soundtrack - and vice versa. At Cornell, I’ll join a Telugu community where I can still share my Telugu soundtracks, discuss dosa recipes, and share memes in Telugu. And in all the other communities I join, I know I’ll be able to share my Telugu background and learn about other Cornellians’ cultures.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked the applicant to explain why they want to attend a specific engineering program and how it aligns with their interests and goals.
Response:
Engineering without practical application is futile. Engineering without creativity is boring. I’ve always tried to balance both, a balance that Cornell Engineering embodies.

When building ABIGAIL, I loved modelling 3 different dimensions rather than the usual two, just one of the many ways I was creative with ABIGAIL’s code. But I knew what made ABIGAIL a worthy project was its impact on Alzheimer's. Cornell Neurotech, a truly unique interdisciplinary research team, seems to have this same creative practicality approach that I took with ABIGAIL. They model neural circuits to understand everything from social interactions to vision. After joining Cornell Neurotech, I’d try to introduce a focus in modelling Tau-Tangles, a key symptom of Alzheimer’s.

I’d also join Cornell’s student-led engineering teams, channeling my rocketry spirit by helping design Cornell’s annual Mars Rover and researching synthetic biology in Cornell’s iGEM team (this year, their topic is Martian plant growth!). I’d also use Cornell Engineering’s Duffield Hall and Upson Hall to work on individual projects, developing new drug-delivery systems in their nanotech spaces, and build physical neural sensors in the maker spaces.

Cornell Engineering is where I can balance my curiosity and creativity with practicality, making things that will change the world.

Prompt: Why do you want to study engineering?
Response: Before I learnt about formal circuit schematics, I was using those same schematics to model neurons at RISE. I learnt about capacitors and resistors in the context of membrane potentials and ion channels, and through basic derivatives and Python, I learnt to model a neuron through voltage equations. Eventually, I derived an index that predicted Parkinson’s likelihood. However, my journey with biocomputation first started during early mornings, coding algorithms that learnt about properties of cancer drugs repurposed for Alzheimer’s, while I learnt about activation and loss functions. My models slowly evolved from predicting properties to generating novel Alzheimer's inhibitors. At the same time, my hands itched to do something more than type, so I led a group to develop a physical device that reduced toxic waste from power plants. I connected tubes and valves, attaching arduino circuit boards and calibrating pH sensors. After ordering parts, testing chemistry in our classrooms, and building through the night, we had an initial testable prototype. Bioengineering combines the physical building my hands love with the mystery of biology my brain loves, allowing me to fight medical problems by creating newer, more efficient, computational software and physical devices.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked the writer to describe a personal motto or philosophy that has significantly influenced their life, detailing specific experiences and growth that resulted from adopting this mindset.
Response:
Just do it. These three words have become a mantra for many, a call to action that transcends the boundaries of sports and spills into every aspect of life. For me, this phrase embodies the essence of perseverance and determination. It reminds me that the only way to achieve my goals is to take that first step, no matter how daunting it may seem.

Growing up, I was always the shy kid in the back of the classroom, hesitant to raise my hand or share my thoughts. I often found myself paralyzed by the fear of failure, convinced that my ideas were not worth sharing. However, as I entered high school, I realized that this mindset was holding me back. I wanted to be more than just a passive observer in my own life; I wanted to be an active participant.

One day, I stumbled upon a flyer for a public speaking club. My heart raced at the thought of standing in front of an audience, but I remembered those three words: Just do it. I took a deep breath and signed up. The first meeting was terrifying. I fumbled over my words and felt the heat of embarrassment wash over me. But as I continued to attend, I began to find my voice. I learned to embrace my mistakes and view them as opportunities for growth rather than reasons to retreat.

With each speech I delivered, I grew more confident. I discovered the power of storytelling and how it could connect me with others. I began to share my experiences, my passions, and my dreams. I realized that my voice mattered, and that by speaking up, I could inspire others to do the same.

This newfound confidence spilled over into other areas of my life. I joined the debate team, where I learned to think critically and articulate my thoughts under pressure. I volunteered for leadership roles in various clubs, pushing myself to take on challenges that once seemed insurmountable. Each time I faced a new obstacle, I reminded myself: Just do it.

Now, as I prepare to embark on the next chapter of my life, I carry this mantra with me. I know that there will be moments of doubt and fear, but I also know that I have the strength to push through. Whether it’s tackling a difficult course, pursuing a passion, or stepping into a leadership role, I am ready to embrace the challenges ahead. Just do it is not just a slogan; it is a philosophy that has transformed my life and will continue to guide me as I strive to make a difference in the world.

Prompt: Discuss one of your personal qualities and share a story, anecdote, or memory of how it helped you make a positive impact on a community. This could be your current community or another community you have engaged.
Response: In the summer of 2024, I boarded the lone corner seat on a mobile hospital van in Puttaparthi, Andhra Pradesh, India. For the next 15 days, we visited 15 different villages, setting up makeshift clinics in schoolyards, treating patients who’d waited weeks for care. I took vitals, and packed medication bags, but my biggest impact was through Telugu translations - I helped elders who didn’t understand the Puttaparthi dialect or English medical terms. While I translated doctors’ scribbled notes, I saw confused villagers. As I heard the difference between each village’s dialect, I knew the villagers didn’t understand the Puttaparthi dialect. On Day 6, I heard an old man tell his daughter in Telugu, “I don’t understand when to take these medicines; the doctors don’t make sense”. In him, I saw myself during my first days in India, unable to understand the dialect, and scared to ask. Back in Seattle, I kept thinking about the communication breakdown. So I designed a sticker system to make dosage instructions more accessible. A sun and food sticker meant “after breakfast,” while a moon and water droplet meant “before bed with water.” I emailed the idea and prototypes to Dr. Narasimhan, the mobile van director, and spoke with volunteer pharmacists to get feedback. Dr. Narasimhan is still hesitant to change the current system. But I keep trying. During volunteering hours, I acted as a makeshift translator. Now, I want to integrate this sticker system so that one day, patients won’t need a translator at all.

Prompt: The Stanford community is deeply curious and driven to learn in and out of the classroom. Reflect on an idea or experience that makes you genuinely excited about learning.
Response:
Used in radios, image and audio data compression, and space signal simplification, the Fourier Transform is a function that tells how much of each frequency is in a certain input signal. But I learnt about the Fourier Transform in a much more unconventional setting: researching my deepest fear, Alzheimer’s based dementia.

Dementia is terrifying. I fear that by forgetting my family, losing muscle memory, and forgetting my past, I’ll lose myself. Luckily, evolution protected humans from dementia by wrapping up our memories in Fourier waves. Each memory is encoded by different waves of synchronized neural activity; only by adding these waves up through a reverse Fourier Transform do we see the full memory. Hence, even if neurons that make up individual waves die out, the entire memory isn’t wiped away. Rather, certain details are lost.

Fourier waves are incredible. They were invented from mathematics (representing polynomials as sums of sine and cosine waves) to explain physics phenomena (heat distribution). Centuries later, we find that evolution beat us to it, inventing this wave breakdown to protect our memories. Each month, I get an update from Google Scholar on Alzheimer’s, and feel an excitement when I see ‘Fourier Transform’ as a keyword. Reading such papers, I’ve decided to join the diverse community of physicists, mathematicians, biologists, and programmers trying to protect the Fourier waves of memory in all of our heads.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked for a description of a student's passion or interest, how it has developed over time, and how their experiences have prepared them for future studies or aspirations in a specific field.
Response:
Throughout my life, I have been driven by a passion for learning and a desire to make a positive impact on the world around me. This journey began in my early childhood, where I found myself captivated by the intricacies of the human brain and the complexities of human behavior. As I grew older, this fascination evolved into a commitment to understanding neuroscience and its potential to improve lives.

In high school, I took my first steps towards this goal by founding the Behind The Brain Blog, where I research and write articles on current developments in neuroscience. This platform has allowed me to share my insights with a wider audience, garnering over 250,000 views across various platforms. I have implemented SEO strategies to increase visibility and engage with readers, including guest interviews with neuroscience professors and researchers. This experience has not only honed my writing skills but has also deepened my understanding of the field.

My academic pursuits have been complemented by hands-on experiences in research and volunteer work. I was fortunate to be selected for the Boston University Research in Science & Engineering (RISE) program, where I derived a novel index for predicting Parkinson’s disease. This experience solidified my desire to contribute to the field of neuroscience through research and innovation.

Additionally, my volunteer work at COPE MultiCare Deaconess Hospital has provided me with invaluable insights into patient care and the healthcare system. Assisting staff with patient care tasks and coordinating onboarding for new volunteers has taught me the importance of empathy and communication in healthcare. My time volunteering in a mobile hospital in India further reinforced this lesson, as I communicated directly with patients in Telugu to provide medical support in rural villages.

I have also engaged in independent research, coding models that predict the efficacy of repurposed anticancer drugs for Alzheimer’s disease treatment and generating novel amyloid-beta inhibitors. These projects have allowed me to apply my knowledge in practical settings and contribute to the ongoing fight against neurodegenerative diseases.

As I look towards the future, I am excited about the prospect of furthering my education in neuroscience and contributing to groundbreaking research. I believe that my experiences, both in the classroom and in the field, have prepared me to take on the challenges of higher education and to make a meaningful impact in the world of neuroscience.

Prompt: The prompt likely asked the writer to reflect on an experience or encounter that led them to question or explore the essence of being human or the nature of human curiosity.
Response:
At Deaconess Hospital, quiet hours are from 10pm to 6am. But on the circular 11th Tower, where most patients are elderly and partially deaf, quiet hours begin at 8:00pm. 8:00pm is also sunset, something Susanne loves to watch. I see her furrow her brow and fidget with her fingers.

“Hi Susanne! Whatcha thinking about?” I ask.
“Why does the sun go down?”
“The sun’s making way for the moon!”
“Am I in my apartment?”
“No, Susanne, you’re in the hospital!”
“Well, what’s wrong with me?”
“Nothing’s wrong, we’re just making sure you’re doing okay.”

That last line is a lie. We had this conversation yesterday. But since Susanne has Alzheimer’s, she doesn’t remember. The topic of the questions varies: the sun, cars, my buzzcut. But each day, I’m struck by how despite being 90 years old, Susanne still asks questions.

“Susanne, looks like it’s 8:30!”
“Oh. Is it time to sleep?”
“Mhmm.”

I walk Susanne back to her room making sure she doesn’t trip over her IV tubing. After she’s sound asleep, I pace the hallways, quietly humming a Telugu melody. My mind begins to jump, from the endless shape-to-shape errors popping up in my Python model, to a recent article on how a paralyzed person played chess with their mind through Neuralink. I scribble thoughts on the back of an empty vital sheet: ideas about possible bug fixes and the start of a Neuralink-based dystopia.

As I continue to scan for a call light, my mind wanders to a neuroscience panel discussion I attended last Saturday. Sitting in the audience, dwarfed by the students around me donning their college apparel, I shakily raised my hand and squeaked out, “What is the problem of the century for neuroscience?” Dr. Nino Ramirez answered, “What makes us human?”

To be human, I used to believe, was within our genes. I thought humanness came from the ARHGAP11B gene which grows our midbrain, allowing our brain to be bigger than other animals’. But thinking about it now, we neither have the largest brain, nor the largest brain-body mass ratio: the sperm whale and the ant take those titles.

As I write on my vital sheet, I cross out “ARHGAP11B”, writing and circling “opposable thumbs” right below. Opposable thumbs allow me to hold my pen and play tennis. But these are a trait of all primates, not just humans. Perhaps it's language that makes us human. But as I write down “language”, I realize that so many animals have complex language: dolphins, whales, elephants, and surprisingly prairie dogs.

“Disculpe!”, says Hernando in Room 1104, calling for my attention. Agua con hielo - ice water, is what he wants, so I cut through the nurses’ desk station to fill up a plastic grey cup. I quickly raise his bed to Fowler’s position (45-60 degrees) so he can drink his water. Hernando doesn’t understand English and the Spanish interpreter is shared between multiple floors, so Hernando is often lonely. So, I talk with him for a bit in Spanish until he falls asleep.

Alone again, my mind begins to wander. Hernando had asked for water, an important survival need. Susanne’s questions, on the other hand, stemmed from epistemic curiosity - questions unrelated to basic survival. I begin to scribble a formal definition of what it means to be human - trying to connect our genetics, language, opposable thumbs, and the midbrain (where epistemic curiosity is expressed) - when my pocket buzzes. My dad is calling me for the 6th time in 10 minutes.

“Siddharth, where are you? Your shift ended 15 minutes ago!”

I quickly stuff the vital sheet into my pocket, and run to the elevators. The elevator descends, and I bolt through the night exit. As I step into the cold dark Spokane night, a thought comes to me: perhaps, what makes us human is our tendency to ask questions in those small quiet hours.
